windows.transcript: pictures Next place your pictures directly on the stove and turn your burners on medium low heat And now you're just going to wait for your wax to melt
windows.embed_text_v2: melting wax: two aluminum pouring pitchers full of solid white coconut wax chunks heating on a gas stovetop burner with blue flame, chunks softening and liquefying over medium-low heat
windows.summary_v2: Two aluminum pitchers of solid white coconut wax sit on a lit gas stovetop burner, slowly melting down over medium-low heat.

seconds_v2.desc: Two aluminum candle-pouring pitchers packed with chunks of solid white coconut wax stand on a gas stovetop burner that has just been lit.
The front pitcher brims with hard white wax chunks; a low blue flame burns beneath it on the grate.
Wax chunks sit unmelted in both metal pitchers as the burner heats them on medium-low.
Heat begins working on the wax; the white chunks in the front pitcher start to look slightly glossy at the edges.
The mound of wax in the front pitcher settles a little as the bottom starts to melt over the flame.
Wax level in the front pitcher drops as more of the solid chunks soften and begin to liquefy.
The white wax has slumped into a creamier, partly melted mass inside the front aluminum pitcher.
Both pitchers show melting wax, surfaces turning smooth and translucent over the burner.
Wax continues melting down in the metal pitchers on the stove, now a soft creamy pool with a few chunks left.

windows.transcript: my stove With coconut wax I like to add fragrance at around 160 degrees Using my thermometer will help me determine when my
windows.embed_text_v2: checking wax temperature: dial thermometer dipped into melted amber coconut wax in a tall aluminum pouring pitcher on a gas stovetop, monitoring heat near 160 degrees before adding fragrance
windows.summary_v2: A maker checks the temperature of melted amber wax in an aluminum pitcher on the stove with a dial thermometer before adding fragrance.

seconds_v2.desc: Close low angle of an aluminum pouring pitcher being placed onto the gas stove grate, a knit cloth in the foreground.
The tall aluminum pitcher of melted amber coconut wax sits on the burner, a second pitcher behind it.
Molten golden wax fills the front pitcher on the stovetop, surface smooth and liquid.
A round dial thermometer is lowered into the melted wax to check its temperature.
The dial thermometer rests in the amber wax, its needle reading the heat of the melt.
Thermometer stays clipped in the pitcher as the maker monitors the wax temperature on the stove.
Steady shot of the dial thermometer in the molten wax, gauging when to add fragrance near 160 degrees.
The thermometer dial reads the rising temperature of the liquid wax in the metal pitcher.
Melted amber wax in the pitcher with the dial thermometer in place, temperature being checked before adding fragrance.

windows.transcript: But you may have some cracks or discrepancies that look like this
windows.embed_text_v2: inspecting cured candles: set jar candles on a wood counter shown in macro with cracks, sinkholes and rough uneven wax surfaces around the wick, pale cream and green coconut wax, defects after cooling
windows.summary_v2: Close-ups of cooled jar candles reveal cracks and uneven, sunken surfaces on the cured wax around the wicks.

seconds_v2.desc: A cluster of set candles in glass mason jars rests on a wood countertop, pale green and cream wax with paper wicks, a potted succulent behind.
Same group of cooled jar candles on the wooden surface, their tops solidified and slightly uneven.
Extreme close-up of two cured candle tops; the left jar shows a rough, crumbly white surface around the wick.
Top-down macro of a cured candle, its pale surface pulled and dimpled near the upright wick.
Close view of a set candle top revealing a shallow sinkhole and rough patches of wax around the wick.
Macro pans across cured candle surfaces showing cracks and lumpy texture beside the wick.
Another jar candle top in close-up with an uneven, frosted surface and a small wax defect near the wick.
Tight shot of two cooled candle tops, surfaces wrinkled and imperfect around the centered wicks.
Final macro of set candle tops, highlighting the rough cured wax and standing wicks in the jars.

windows.transcript: If this happens all you need to do is reheat your leftover wax to 150 degrees
windows.embed_text_v2: reheating leftover wax: aluminum pouring pitchers placed back on a lit gas stovetop burner to remelt leftover coconut wax to 150 degrees to fix cracks and sinkholes in cured candles
windows.summary_v2: After showing candle defects, leftover wax is reheated in aluminum pitchers on the gas stove to fix the cracked candle tops.

seconds_v2.desc: Macro of a cured jar candle top showing a small sinkhole and rough wax around the wick, last frame of the defect shot.
Cut to two aluminum pouring pitchers set back on the lit gas stove to reheat leftover wax, a low flame beneath the front pitcher.
The tall aluminum pitcher sits on the burner grate with a little leftover wax in the bottom, warming up.
Steady shot of the metal pitcher on the stove flame as the leftover wax begins to reheat.
The pitcher continues heating on the burner, a second pitcher behind it on the grate.
A hand reaches to a stir stick in the rear pitcher while the front pitcher reheats over the flame.
Leftover wax warms in the aluminum pitcher on the gas burner, the maker tending the back pitcher.
The front pitcher stays centered on the flame, leftover wax melting again toward 150 degrees.
Aluminum pitcher of reheating leftover wax on the stove, a hand adjusting the second pitcher behind it.

tags_v2.action: showing the smooth set tops of finished jar candles

seconds_v2.desc: Extreme close-up of a clear glass jar candle with a smooth white set wax surface and an upright braided wick standing in the centre.
Same jar candle, camera slightly closer on the glossy cream-white solid wax top and centred wick.
Close view across the rim of the filled jar, smooth pale wax surface, second jar blurred behind.
Camera pans over the jar's threaded glass neck showing the cured white wax and wick base.
Tight shot tilting across the tops of two finished white jar candles set side by side.
Close-up of the wick rising from the smooth white wax in the clear jar, warm wood surface behind.
Macro view of the centred wick and faintly rippled white set wax inside the glass jar.
The jar candle's smooth cream wax top and wick fill the frame, another jar edge at lower right.
Final close framing of the finished white jar candle, even wax surface and upright wick.
